stem: A automotive testing study measures the fuel consumption (L/100km) for a sample of 20 vehicles. The sample mean is 190.4 L/100km with a sample standard deviation of 44.6 L/100km. However, 2 of the 20 observations appear to be outliers (values more than 2.5 standard deviations from the mean). If these outliers are removed, the remaining 18 observations have a mean of 182.9 L/100km and standard deviation of 27.4 L/100km. Estimate the true population mean and provide a 95% confidence interval that accounts for the uncertainty about whether outliers should be included.

ground_truth.answer: With all data: mean = 190.4, 95% CI = [169.5, 211.3]. Without outliers: mean = 182.9, 95% CI = [170.0, 195.8]. Combined interval accounting for analytic uncertainty: [169.5, 211.3]. Point estimate: 186.7 L/100km.

solution_steps: The outlier treatment choice creates structural uncertainty. Combined interval: [169.5, 211.3].
common_failure_modes: Using only the full sample without considering outlier impact
Removing outliers without reporting both analyses
Not recognizing that the choice of analysis method is itself a source of uncertainty
